common_name: Diamond Head Elephant Ear (Compact Charcoal-Black Colocasia)

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Diamond Head is a self-heading cultivar — it does not produce the long runners (stolons) that spread many Colocasia cultivars aggressively through garden beds.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to partial sun; Diamond Head is a relatively compact, self-heading dark elephant ear — producing large, heart-shaped, dramatically dark charcoal-to-purple-black leaves similar to 'Black Magic' but on a more compact, controlled plant (3–4 feet rather than 4–6 feet) that doesn't spread aggressively by stolons as many Colocasia cultivars do. The 'self-heading' growth habit is Diamond Head's most practically valuable attribute for garden design: while vigorous Colocasia cultivars like 'Black Magic' spread through underground stolons to create expanding colonies (a quality that is sometimes desirable and sometimes invasive in garden beds), Diamond Head stays as a compact, tidy clump that can be managed like a conventional perennial. The exceptionally dark, glossy leaves — more charcoal than the pure-black of 'Black Magic' and with a distinctive glossy sheen — create a bold tropical accent that is dramatic without the unpredictability of running types. In zone 9, Diamond Head is reliably perennial, dying back in cold winters but returning from persistent tubers in spring. The compact habit makes it outstanding for mixed borders, container plantings, and defined landscape spaces where a controllable but dramatic dark tropical accent is desired.

common_name: Polly Alocasia (African Mask Plant with Bold White Veining)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Polly Alocasia requires warm temperatures (above 65°F) to thrive — plant outdoors only after night temperatures are consistently above 60°F.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Polly is a compact cultivar of Alocasia × amazonica (the African Mask Plant) — one of the most dramatically graphic foliage plants available, producing arrowhead to shield-shaped leaves 10–16 inches long in deep, glossy, blackish-dark-green with bold, contrasting, creamy-white to ivory-white veins and leaf margins creating a striking graphic pattern reminiscent of tribal masks or stained glass. The contrast between the very dark, glossy leaf surface and the crisp white veins is among the most visually dramatic foliage patterns of any commonly available ornamental plant. Polly's compact size (18–24 inches) makes it more practical for typical garden use than the larger Alocasia × amazonica species (which can reach 4–6 feet). As an outdoor plant in zone 9, Polly performs as a warm-season tropical that requires warm soil and air temperatures — plant outdoors from late April through early October and bring containers indoors before the first frost, or protect in-ground plants with heavy mulch. In frost-free portions of zone 9b and 10, Polly can remain outdoors year-round. The dramatic foliage creates an exotic, jungle-garden aesthetic that is outstanding in large containers, as a shade garden focal point, or in tropical-themed garden designs. Avoid cold drafts, direct sun, and dry soil.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost date when soil has warmed to at least 65°F; plant 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Moonlight's chartreuse-to-cream coloration makes it one of the brightest caladiums for deep shade illumination.

directions: Plant tubers in spring after the last frost in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Moonlight is a large-leaved, predominantly chartreuse to cream-white caladium with an unusual warm, yellow-green to near-white leaf color that creates one of the brightest and most luminous foliage effects of any fancy caladium cultivar. The warm, slightly yellow-tinged white-to-cream color differs from the pure, cool white of 'Candidum' — Moonlight has a warmer, softer quality that creates a glowing, moonlit effect particularly striking in the dim light of deep-shade garden areas at dusk and in low-light conditions. In brighter partial shade, the color is a vivid chartreuse-green that gradually lightens toward cream-white at the center of each leaf as temperatures rise through summer. The large leaf size and predominantly light-colored foliage maximize the light-reflective effect in shade positions — Moonlight is an outstanding choice for illuminating dark corners and brightening shaded foundation areas. The chartreuse-cream color contrasts beautifully with dark-foliaged companions like Black Magic elephant ear, 'Obsidian' heuchera, or dark ajuga groundcovers, creating a vivid light-dark foliage combination. Consistent moisture, humus-rich soil, and protection from direct afternoon sun produce the largest leaves and best color in Gulf South conditions.

common_name: Mystic Spires Blue Sage (Long-Blooming Compact Indigo Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost date in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mystic Spires Blue blooms on new growth — light shearing after each main flush encourages continuous flower production through the entire growing season.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost date; Mystic Spires Blue is one of the most successful and widely planted compact salvias for the Gulf South — a hybrid between Salvia farinacea and S. longispicata that produces long (6–10 inch), dense, indigo-blue flower spikes on a more compact plant (2–3 feet) than the related 'Indigo Spires' (3–5 feet), making it proportionate for typical residential border and container use. The indigo-blue flower color — a deep, saturated blue-violet that maintains its intensity through the Gulf South summer — is among the most valuable flower colors for sun gardens, where true blue is scarce. Mystic Spires Blue blooms continuously from late spring through hard frost in zone 9 — the long bloom season (May through November or longer) and compact habit combine to make it one of the most useful and ornamentally effective salvias for the Gulf South. The deep blue flowers are highly attractive to a wide range of pollinators — bees of many species, gulf fritillary and monarch butterflies, and hummingbirds all visit regularly. Plants are drought-tolerant once established, tolerating clay, heat, and humidity without significant decline. In zone 9, Mystic Spires Blue may be killed by severe cold but the tuberous roots often survive mild zone 9 winters and resprout in spring. A stellar three-season performer for the Gulf South full-sun border.

common_name: Aji Amarillo Pepper (Fruity Orange-Yellow Peruvian Hot Pepper)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Aji Amarillo has a long season to maturity (90–120 days from transplant) — in zone 9, the long warm season supports full production from spring transplants.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Aji Amarillo is the foundational hot pepper of Peruvian cuisine — considered one of the essential 'holy trinity' ingredients of Peruvian cooking (with garlic and onion) and the basis for Peru's iconic yellow sauces. The 3–5 inch, elongated-conical, thin-walled fruits ripen from green through orange-yellow to bright yellow-orange, with a distinctive fruity, tropical flavor and significant heat (30,000–50,000 Scoville units) that is entirely different from the neutral burn of jalapeños. The fruity, complex flavor — often described as combining apricot, mango, and passion fruit with a persistent, building heat — makes Aji Amarillo irreplaceable in Peruvian cooking and increasingly prized by chefs worldwide for its unique flavor profile. As a Capsicum baccatum species (the Andean species group distinct from C. annuum and C. chinense), Aji Amarillo requires somewhat warmer germination temperatures and a longer season than standard bell peppers. In zone 9, the long growing season is ideal for the 90–120 day maturity period, and the plants may become quite large (3–4 feet) and productive by mid-summer. Fresh fruits, paste, and dried/powdered form all have distinct culinary applications. The dried, rehydrated paste is the standard form used in traditional Peruvian cooking.
